context.description: The text argues that an acute threat can trigger an involuntary bodily stress cascade that rapidly overrides conscious control. It uses this example to show how emotions and their triggers operate beyond deliberate will, even though training can reduce symptoms and improve response. The passage concludes that this level of bodily mastery exceeds what conscious effort can produce.
normalization_notes: Source language is German; summary translated into English.
No chapter heading visible on the page.

rows.paragraph_text: An acute threat triggers an involuntary stress cascade that rapidly changes heart rate, breathing-related oxygen delivery, sweating, vision, and perception while the body scans for escape or attack options.
The passage argues that conscious effort cannot match this automatic bodily control, even with extensive military or martial training.
Training may reduce the symptoms and preserve clear thinking, but it cannot create or directly trigger the underlying cascade by will alone.
The example is used to conclude that people are not above their emotions or their triggers.
